channel.title: Functional patterns on OOFP 2025-26
channel.description: Recent content in Functional patterns on OOFP 2025-26

item.title: Fold
item.description:
Fold #

A variety of operations on a list can be understood as combining its values inductively, either from left to right or from right to left.

Example. Consider the list

$\qquad 1, 2, 3, 4$

The sum of its elements can be computed inductively as:

  • $((1 + 2) + 3) + 4\qquad$ (left-to-right), or
  • $1 + (2 + (3 + 4))\qquad$ (right-to-left)

Similarly, the least element in this list can be computed as

item.title: Pipeline
item.description:
Pipeline #

Motivation #

Syntax. Consider a chain of function applications, where the output of each function is the (unique) input to the next function in the chain. For instance

$\qquad h(g(f(x)))$

Computationally, this reads from right to left :

$\qquad$ “start with $x$, then apply $f$ to it, then apply $g$ to the result, and then $h$ to the result”.

Instead, when we write code, it can be convenient to use an expression that mimics the order of execution (when read from left to right).

item.title: Functor
item.description:
Functor #

The notion of functor comes from a branch of mathematics called category theory.

For programmers, a functor can be viewed as a parameterized type that implements a certain function, while respecting certain constraints.

In Haskell #

Notation. We will use t in this section to refer to a Haskell functor.

Definition. A functor in Haskell is a parameterized type t that implements a function

$\qquad$ fmap :: (a -> b) -> t a -> t b

item.title: Monad
item.description:
Monad #

Like functors, monads come from category theory.

For programmers, a monad can be viewed as a functor that implements additional functions, while respecting certain constraints.

In Haskell #

We have seen in the previous section that a functor is a parameterized type that implements the function fmap.

A monad is a functor that implements two additional functions, called return and join.

Example. We have seen how the parameterized type “List” (written [ ] in Haskell) can be made a functor by implementing fmap.
